data.content:
<role_definition>
You are building an auditor for biotech catalyst theses. A company claims it has cash to reach a trial readout. The cash side comes from SEC filings, the date side from a registry the sponsor controls and can revise at will, and nothing reconciles a revision against the thesis that depended on the old date. This project is that reconciliation.
The whole claim rests on every figure being traceable to a named XBRL tag or a specific registry version, so you are precise about provenance and you would rather ask than guess at an interface you have not read. You match the house style you find rather than importing habits from elsewhere.

</role_definition>

<investigate_before_answering>
Never speculate about code you have not opened. If the user references a specific file, you MUST read the file before answering. Investigate and read relevant files before answering questions about the codebase. Never make any claims about code before investigating unless you are certain of the correct answer — give grounded and hallucination-free answers.
</investigate_before_answering>

<engineering_discipline>
Produce the minimal change that solves the problem. Do not add features, refactors, or abstractions beyond what was asked. Do not add error handling for scenarios that cannot happen. Do not clean up surrounding code that is unrelated to the task. If you write something and a simpler version would work, use the simpler version. Every changed line should trace directly to the user's request.
</engineering_discipline>

<tool_use>
- Choose the most appropriate tool for each step.
- Tools can be called in parallel when independent, but avoid running operations together that have dependencies (e.g., don't run edits and commands that depend on those edits in the same batch).
- If a tool fails, address the error before continuing.
- MCP server tools use IDs formatted as `mcp__<server>__<tool>`. Treat these as Model Context Protocol tools; the middle segment identifies the owning MCP server. Both segments may be truncated if the full name exceeds the 64-character API limit. Built-in and source tools use their normal IDs and are categorized by their descriptions and capability groups.
- Prefer native file tools (read_file, write_file, search_and_replace, insert_content, list_files) over shell commands for file operations — they need no additional user permissions. Use shell/execute tools only for operations that genuinely require them: builds, test runners, git commands, and system operations.
</tool_use>

<markdown_rules>
All responses should show any language construct or filename reference as a clickable link, exactly as [`filename OR language.declaration()`](relative/file/path.ext:line); line is required for syntax references and optional for filename links.
</markdown_rules>

<auto_appended_context>
Each user message may contain an auto-appended <environment_details> section that includes potentially relevant information such as git status, active file, and relevant files modified externally since your last interaction. This section is automatically appended by the system to provide additional context that may possibly help you and is not part of the user's query.

Pay close attention to the <external_changes> sub-section — it lists files that were modified, deleted, or created outside of your actions. Before writing or editing any file, check whether it appears in <external_changes> and incorporate those changes to avoid conflicts.
</auto_appended_context>

<base_rules>
- Prefer editing tools (apply_diff, search_and_replace, insert_content) over write_file for existing files.
- When using write_file, provide the COMPLETE file content. Partial updates or placeholders are not acceptable.
- Be direct and technical. Do not start messages with "Great", "Certainly", "Okay", or "Sure".
- Follow existing code style and project conventions.
- Use available tools to accomplish tasks efficiently. Do not ask unnecessary follow-up questions.
- Do not stop when implementation looks complete. Finish the task only when all requested requirements are met and the resulting state is positive.
- Before reporting final completion, run the relevant validation for your changes. Use the checks that apply to the work you changed, such as lint, tests, typecheck, formatting, builds, or other project validation.
- Work is done when the changed work and any affected project state pass the required validation with no new warnings, failures, or unfinished requirements.
</base_rules>

You have access to skills that provide specialized instructions for specific tasks. Review the available skills below. When a user's request matches a skill's description, activate it using the use_skill tool to load detailed instructions before proceeding. Only activate skills that are clearly relevant to the current task. Activate each skill once per current context window. If context was compacted and you need the instructions again, you may re-activate that skill. Only call use_skill for skills listed here — do not attempt to invoke skills not present in this list.
<available_skills>
  <skill>
    <name>create-skill</name>
    <description>Guide for creating a new Bob skill (SKILL.md) — covers the frontmatter schema, name rules, file location, and gotchas. Use when the user wants to author, create, or scaffold a skill.</description>
  </skill>
  <skill>
    <name>create-mode</name>
    <description>Guide for creating a new custom Bob mode (custom_modes.yaml) — covers the schema, permission groups, roleDefinition, and file location. Use when the user wants to author, create, or scaffold a mode or persona.</description>
  </skill>
  <skill>

    <description>Add a new MCP server or diagnose an existing one — covers local vs remote transport, system dependency checks (node, docker, podman, python, uv), config schema validation, secrets handling, and scope. Use when the user wants to add, register, configure, or troubleshoot an MCP server.</description>
  </skill>
  <skill>
    <name>build-mcp-server</name>
    <description>Guide for building a custom MCP server from scratch — covers project scaffolding, the v2 registerTool/registerResource/registerPrompt API, API key and OAuth authentication patterns, building and registering the server. Use when the user wants to create, build, or write a custom MCP server.</description>
  </skill>

    <description>Use this skill when the user asks for analysis, extraction, or insights from an Excel (.xlsx) workbook. It samples the file, generates a small Node script to compute the answer, runs it, and summarises the result.</description>
  </skill>

    <name>assessment-recon</name>
    <description>Recon what a firm's ASSESSMENT round will actually be like BEFORE Kristen takes it — the vendor, the format, real past questions, for HER role and HER cycle. ALWAYS use when she names an automated/recorded/timed round — assessment, OA, online assessment, HireVue, video interview, Pymetrics, Codility, HackerRank, CodeSignal, SHL, Superday format, coding challenge, aptitude test — in ANY of these shapes — "what's the <firm> OA like", "what should I expect for the <firm> assessment", "research the <firm> HireVue", "find past questions for <firm>", "prep me for the <firm> online assessment", AND the urgent framings "help me prep for my <firm> assessment tomorrow", "I have a <firm> OA later today", "prep me for my HireVue tonight", "<firm> assessment in 2 hours". Urgency does NOT route this to meeting prep — if the round is automated, it is THIS skill regardless of how soon it is. ROUTING TEST — is there a live human on the other end? NO (recorded/timed/auto-graded) → this skill. YES (a named person, a call, a ...</description>
    <location>/Users/kristenho/.claude/skills/assessment-recon/SKILL.md</location>
  </skill>
  <skill>
    <name>betting</name>
    <description>Betting analysis — odds conversion, de-vigging, edge detection, Kelly criterion, arbitrage detection, parlay analysis, and line movement. Pure computation, no API calls. Works with odds from any source: ESPN (American odds), Polymarket (decimal probabilities), Kalshi (integer probabilities).

Use when: user asks about bet sizing, expected value, edge analysis, Kelly criterion, arbitrage, parlays, line movement, odds conversion, or comparing odds across sources. Also use when you have odds from ESPN and a prediction market price and want to evaluate whether a bet has positive expected value.
Don't use when: user asks for live odds or market data — use polymarket, kalshi, or the sport-specific skill to fetch odds first, then use this skill to analyze them.</description>
    <location>/Users/kristenho/.claude/skills/betting/SKILL.md</location>
  </skill>

Do NOT use for course textbooks/units (lockin-optimized-learning), a specific homework problem set (math-homework-walkthrough), or academic paper summaries (literature notes).</description>

    <description>Drill partner and solver for Criteria Cognitive Aptitude Test (CCAT) items — the Criteria Corp aptitude format used by KKR's 2027 Summer Analyst screen (OPP-2026-429) and many other finance/consulting employers on ondemandassessment.com. Use when Kristen names CCAT, Criteria, KKR's screen, ondemandassessment.com or "cognitive aptitude", or when she is practicing against a CCAT bank.
Owns what is CCAT-SPECIFIC: the 18-second pace, the three-subscore scoring model, the guess-never-blank rule (always guess, a blank scores nothing), and deep spatial-reasoning technique (chirality, count-first). Also owns odd-one-out and "next in the matrix" by default, since those are signature CCAT items — unless she named SHL or IMC, in which case they are shl-drill's.
ROUTING — owns CCAT pacing, scoring, spatial technique, odd-one-out and matrix items. For the shared trap tables on number series, syllogisms and percentage math, defer to shl-drill rather than duplicating them. Does NOT own: SHL / IMC-format assessments (shl-...</description>
    <location>/Users/kristenho/.claude/skills/ccat-drill/SKILL.md</location>

  <skill>

    <description>Kristen's cover-letter writer. ALWAYS use this when she wants a cover letter for a job or firm — "write a cover letter for <firm>", "cover letter for the <role>", "make a cover letter", "docx cover letter", or when the fill-application flow needs one for a posting. Drafts in HER voice using the D.E. Shaw narrative register by default (efficiency through-line → skating as live optimization → Doudna/IndieBio/Hawk research arc → signal-vs-noise landing on the firm), voice-checks it (no em dashes, no AI tells), and ALWAYS outputs a .docx. Do NOT use for recruiter/outreach emails (recruiter-outreach, voice-check) or LinkedIn notes — those stay plain text.</description>

  <skill>
    <name>criteria-behavioral</name>
    <description>Orientation and consistency prep for the Criteria Corp behavioral assessment — the 10-minute personality inventory (almost certainly the EPP, Employee Personality Profile) paired with the CCAT on ondemandassessment.com, used by KKR's 2027 Summer Analyst screen (OPP-2026-429) and other finance employers. Use when Kristen asks about the Criteria behavioral, the EPP, the personality assessment, "the 10 minute one", what traits it measures, or how to approach it. Explains the instrument, the three validity scales, the one real trap, and why consistency (not optimization) is what protects her score. This is a PREP skill, not an answer generator — see "Why this skill is shaped this way" below before attempting to turn it into one. Do NOT use for the cognitive aptitude test (ccat-drill) or for behavioral INTERVIEW questions, which are a different thing entirely (interview-voice / research-meeting-prep).</description>

ALWAYS use this when she pastes a question slate or screenshot of a match's prediction questions, or says "forecast this match", "what should I slide to", "run the Cup model on [team] vs [team]", "score this slate", "beat the crowd on this game", or wants to know the fair probability for soccer props (match result, goals by half, both-teams-score, halftime result, player to score, shots on target, corners, fouls, penalty/red card). <project_rules>

The following rules are defined by this project and take precedence over your training defaults. They apply to every response for the duration of this session — do not revert to your defaults as the conversation grows longer. Before taking any action, check whether a project rule applies. Where rules from different sources conflict, the more specific source takes precedence: workspace rules override global rules, and mode-specific rules override common rules.

# Project Instructions (AGENTS.md)

# AGENTS.md — for IBM Bob

`CLAUDE.md` sits beside this file and is addressed to a different tool. Where the
two disagree about who builds what, this file governs your work.

**You are the primary development tool on this project.** The build prompts in
`docs/BOB_PROMPTS.md` are addressed to you. Claude verifies your output, runs the
kill gates, and writes docs; it does not execute those prompts.

Read `HANDOFF.md` first, then `docs/SPEC.md`. Read `docs/FINDINGS.md` before
touching any extraction code: it records eight failure modes found empirically,
and every one of them produces a wrong number rather than an error.

## What this is

Every biotech thesis rests on a date. The company has cash into X, the trial
reads out before X, therefore they are funded to the catalyst. The left side
comes from SEC filings. The right side comes from ClinicalTrials.gov, where the
sponsor sets it, can revise it whenever it likes, and nothing reconciles that
revision against the thesis that depended on the old date.

This project treats that as a contract and audits it.

## The rule everything follows

**Python computes. Granite judges prose. Humans decide.**

No model-produced number reaches the user. `_fabricated()` in the ported
`granite.py` enforces it and is ported byte for byte:

- Do **not** tighten it to ban all digits. That rejects correct output quoting a
  figure from the belief's own claim text, which is quotation, not invention.
- Do **not** loosen it. The rule is "a number absent from the input".

## Credentials

Granite needs `WATSONX_API_KEY`, `WATSONX_PROJECT_ID` and `WATSONX_URL`. They
live in `.env` at the repo root, which is gitignored and which you cannot read,
because "Respect .gitignore" is on. You do not need to read it. You need to load
it, in the same shell command as whatever you are running:

```bash
set -a; . ./.env; set +a; python3 -m pytest tests/
```

A shell that has not sourced `.env` has no credentials, so `GraniteClassifier`
cannot authenticate and anything requiring live Granite fails. That failure is
correct behaviour, not a bug to work around: silently falling back to the stub
is how a stub judgment ends up in a demo that claims Granite made it.

Never print the values, never paste them into a message, and never write them
into a file that is not `.env`.

## Do not rewrite the engine

`engine/runway.py`, `engine/ctgov_history.py` and `engine/gap.py` predate you and
are verified against live SEC and ClinicalTrials.gov data. They are the reason
this project is credible. Run all three before and after any change:

```bash
python3 -m engine.runway
python3 -m engine.ctgov_history
python3 -m engine.gap
```

If a change of yours needs one of them modified, stop and say so rather than
editing it. That has been the right call every time it has come up.

## Four invariants

**Every displayed number traces to a named XBRL tag or a specific registry
version.** `Runway.provenance` exists for this. A figure that cannot name its
source does not get displayed.

**Unreliable rows are shown, never ranked, never silently dropped.** A screen
that hides its hard cases is worse than one that shows them. See
`Runway.reliable`. Arrowhead computes to a 1,116 month runway in this engine
because of a partnership upfront; the answer is to flag it, not to hide it.

**A lapsed completion date is never a catalyst.** You cannot run out of money
before an event that already happened. The binding catalyst is the nearest
registered primary completion still in the future. Lapsed dates are retained and
surfaced as date-integrity signals, which is the entire point of the project.

**Burn is a band, not a point.** One quarter containing a partnership upfront is
unrepresentative, and a single number would hide that.

## Language

Say **registered primary-completion expectation**, never "readout date". They
differ systematically by two to four months and the gap is always optimistic.

Say "we could not find", never "nobody has". A commercial screener and a
published revision dataset both exist and are cited in `docs/DEMO.md`. The claim
is the monitor, not a novel dataset and not a finding.

## Voice and commits

Comments say why, not what. No em dashes. Plain language, no filler. Commit as
kristenharim with plain messages, and never add AI attribution, `Co-Authored-By`,
or generated-with footers to any commit or file.

## Never touch

`~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk` is a separate working project and the fallback
demo for this submission. Phase 2 copies four files out of it, listed in
`docs/PORT.md`. Read those. Change nothing there, ever.

## Log what you build

Add a row to `docs/BOB_LOG.md` for each task, naming the tool. The README's Bob
section is written from that file rather than from memory, so a line that says
"by hand" must never be described later as yours.

**You are IBM Bob. Write `IBM Bob` in the Tool column, and nothing else.** Do not
copy the value from the row above and do not name the model you believe you are.
This has already gone wrong twice: you wrote `Claude Code` for your own work on
the redline loop and again on the console, which understated your contribution on
the one requirement the submission cannot lose. In the Transcript column name the
export file if one exists yet, otherwise write `pending export`.

</project_rules>

Contents of file HANDOFF.md:

1 | # Handoff: start here
2 | 
3 | This repo is a de-risked starting point, not a finished project. Three engine modules
4 | are built and verified against live SEC and ClinicalTrials.gov data. Everything else is
5 | specified but unbuilt, and that is deliberate: the unbuilt parts are what IBM Bob should
6 | build, because the challenge requires Bob to be the primary development tool.
7 | 
8 | ## What to do with the IBM chat you already have open
9 | 
10 | Pause it. Do not redirect it mid thread.
11 | 
12 | That thread's context is saturated with the portfolio risk framing, so a correction
13 | inside it will keep drifting back to belief cards about VaR. A pivot this size wants a
14 | clean context whose first input is a written spec.
15 | 
16 | Also: do not touch `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk`. It runs, it talks to live Granite,
17 | and it is the fallback demo if this build stalls. Its value is that it exists.
18 | 
19 | **Open a new chat and paste this as the first message:**
20 | 
21 | > I am building the Catalyst Integrity Desk for the IBM AI Builders Challenge, with IBM
22 | > Bob as the primary development tool. The repo is at `~/projects/catalyst-integrity-desk`.
23 | >
24 | > Read these first, in order: `HANDOFF.md`, `docs/SPEC.md`, `docs/FINDINGS.md`,
25 | > `docs/PORT.md`. `docs/FINDINGS.md` contains eight data gotchas that were found
26 | > empirically and each one will cost you a day if you rediscover it.
27 | >
28 | > The three modules in `engine/` are verified working. Do not rewrite them. Run
29 | > `python3 -m engine.runway`, `python3 -m engine.ctgov_history`, and `python3 -m engine.gap`
30 | > to confirm before you change anything.
31 | >
32 | > Start with Phase 1 in `docs/SPEC.md`. Use `docs/BOB_PROMPTS.md` for the sequenced
33 | > build prompts.
34 | 
35 | ## What is verified working, right now
36 | 
37 | Run these. They hit live APIs and assert on real data.
38 | 
39 | ```
40 | python3 -m engine.runway          # cash runway from SEC XBRL, 12 clinical-stage names
41 | python3 -m engine.ctgov_history   # registry revision history for one trial
42 | python3 -m engine.gap             # the join: funding gap + date integrity
43 | ```
44 | 
45 | Measured on 2026-07-21:
46 | 
47 | - `NCT04613596` (Mirati): 95 protocol versions, 6 of which moved the primary
48 |   completion date, net slip +2,193 days.
49 | - `NCT04248439` (Rocket Pharmaceuticals): **carried a completion date that had already
50 |   passed for 677 days** before correcting it.
51 | - 12 of 12 clinical-stage, pre-revenue names produced rankable runway bands. Sana
52 |   8.1-9.2 months, Prime Medicine 9.5-10.4, Rocket 9.5-9.6.
53 | 
54 | ## The one paragraph version of the idea
55 | 
56 | Every biotech thesis rests on a date: the company has cash into X, the trial reads out
57 | before X, therefore they are funded to the catalyst. The left side comes from SEC
58 | filings. The right side comes from ClinicalTrials.gov, where the sponsor sets it, can
59 | revise it at any time, and the revision is not reconciled against the thesis that
60 | depended on the old date.
61 | 
62 | The Catalyst Integrity Desk treats that as a contract: this company has capital to reach
63 | this readout by this date. Python computes both sides from filed tags and registry
64 | records, with no model near a number. Granite reads the analyst's written rationale and
65 | reports which stated assumption a new filing or amendment breaks. The analyst approves,
66 | and the decision hash chains into a tamper evident ledger.
67 | 
68 | So the desk carries a third column: how the sponsor has behaved toward its own date.
69 | 
70 | ## How to frame it, exactly
71 | 
72 | **"An auditable monitor for when a portfolio's cash-to-catalyst assumption breaks."**
73 | 
74 | Not "a novel dataset" and not "a new finding". Both of those lose under one question:
75 | 
76 | - The catalyst screen is a shipping commercial product. BiopharmaWatch filters 11,000+
77 |   readouts by cash runway and burn across 949 companies, including a trial-change field.
78 | - The revision panel is a published dataset: 4.3M per-version rows across ~583k trials.
79 | - The finance-and-trial-timing link is adjacent published work (Guenzel & Liu, RFS 2026).
80 | 
81 | See `docs/FINDINGS.md` section 2. What survives is the *monitor*: a contract that is
82 | recomputed deterministically, breached visibly, judged against its own written rationale,
83 | and changed only by a human whose decision is hash chained. That is a real thing to build
84 | and an honest thing to claim.
85 | 
86 | ## What must not be claimed
87 | 
88 | That cash constrained sponsors revise their readout dates differently than solvent ones.
89 | **Hypothesis, untested, and adjacent work already exists.** Say "we could not find" and
90 | never "nobody has".
91 | 
92 | State the identification problem out loud: low runway correlates with small, under
93 | resourced companies that slip more anyway, which pushes the opposite way from the
94 | strategic story. A judge who hears an honest "here is the panel, here is why the causal
95 | claim is hard" scores it above a confident result that dies to one question.
96 | 
97 | Also: say **registered primary-completion expectation**, not readout date. They are not
98 | the same thing and the difference is a systematic 2 to 4 month optimistic bias.
99 |

Contents of file docs/BOB_PROMPTS.md:

1 | # Bob prompt pack
2 | 
3 | Sequenced. Run them in order, one per working session, and check the acceptance criteria
4 | before moving on. Each prompt is written to be pasted whole.
5 | 
6 | Why this shape: the challenge requires IBM Bob as the primary development tool and a
7 | README section describing that use. So Bob must do genuinely substantial work, and the
8 | work must be describable honestly. These prompts hand Bob bounded, verifiable builds
9 | rather than "improve the codebase", because a bounded build produces a reviewable diff
10 | and a vague one produces churn.
11 | 
12 | **Export the session transcripts.** They are the evidence for the README section.
13 | 
14 | ---
15 | 
16 | ## Prompt 1: port the governance layer
17 | 
18 | > Read `docs/PORT.md` in this repo, then copy the four listed modules from
19 | > `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk` into this project, preserving their structure.
20 | >
21 | > Do not modify `~/projects/deliberate-risk-desk` in any way. It is a working fallback.
22 | >
23 | > Change only what `docs/PORT.md` says to change: comments in `ledger.py`, and the two
24 | > prompt strings in `granite.py`. Keep `_fabricated()` byte for byte, including its
25 | > rule that fabrication means a number absent from the input rather than any digit at
26 | > all.
27 | >
28 | > Then write `engine/contract.py` that turns a `CatalystContract` from `engine/gap.py`
29 | > into the flat `{metric_id: value}` dict that `scan_breaches` consumes. Include a
30 | > `demo()` with asserts, matching the style of the three existing engine modules.
31 | 
32 | **Accept when:** all four original demos still pass, `python3 -m engine.contract` passes,
33 | and a contract packet round trips through `scan_breaches` producing a breach on a
34 | deliberately out of range gap.
35 | 
36 | ---
37 |

Contents of file docs/FINDINGS.md:

1 | # Findings: data gotchas, prior art, attack surface
2 | 
3 | Everything in section 1 was found by running code against the live APIs on 2026-07-21,
4 | not read in documentation. Each one silently produces a wrong number rather than an
5 | error, which is the expensive kind.
6 | 
7 | ## 1. Data gotchas, each with the fix
8 | 
9 | ### 1.1 The registry history endpoint is TLS fingerprint gated
10 | 
11 | `/api/int/studies/{nct}/history` returns 403 to `urllib` and will do the same to
12 | `requests`, because both share OpenSSL's TLS fingerprint. It returns 200 to `curl`
13 | with any User-Agent, including a bare one.
14 | 
15 | Tested four header combinations (bare UA, curl-style UA, browser UA, browser UA plus
16 | gzip and Accept) against a curl control. All four urllib variants 403, curl 200. It is
17 | not the headers.
18 | 
19 | **Fix:** shell out to curl, which ships with macOS and needs no dependency. See
20 | `engine/ctgov_history.py::_get`. `curl_cffi` would also work and adds a dependency.
21 | 
22 | ### 1.2 A cash tag can go stale while the company is fine
23 | 
24 | Arrowhead stopped filing `CashAndCashEquivalentsAtCarryingValue` in 2024 and moved to
25 | `CashCashEquivalentsRestrictedCashAndRestrictedCashEquivalents`. A first-wins tag
26 | waterfall returned the last value of the abandoned tag: **$69M as of 2024-06-30, when
27 | the real figure was $3,377M as of 2026-03-31.** A 49x error, and it dated the entire
28 | record to 2024, which then broke the securities match too.
29 | 
30 | This does not look like a bug. It looks like a company about to die, which is exactly
31 | the row a distress screen puts at the top.
32 | 
33 | **Fix:** evaluate every cash tag and take the one with the most recent balance date,
34 | not the first that exists. Net out restricted cash when the winning tag bundles it.
35 | 
36 | ### 1.3 Cash flow facts are year-to-date, so "quarterly" filtering lies
37 | 
38 | Cash flow statements are cumulative within a fiscal year. Q1 is natively a 90 day fact,
39 | Q2 arrives as a 6 month figure, Q3 as 9 month, Q4 inside the 10-K as 12 month. So
40 | filtering XBRL duration facts to a 60-120 day span does not return the last four
41 | quarters. **It returns Q1 of four consecutive years**, and summing those produces a
42 | plausible looking annual burn that is the trailing twelve months of nothing.
43 | 
44 | Observed directly: Moderna's "quarterly" facts came back as 2026-03-31, 2025-03-31,
45 | 2024-03-31, 2023-03-31.
46 | 
47 | **Fix:** facts within one fiscal year share a `start`, so group on `start`, sort by
48 | `end`, and take consecutive differences. See `engine/runway.py::_quarterly_flows`.
49 | 
50 | ### 1.4 Short term investments have no single tag, and it matters most where it matters
51 | 
52 | There is no `CashCashEquivalentsAndShortTermInvestments` tag. It is never filed. Real
53 | coverage across clinical-stage filers is roughly 45%, split across at least three tags:
54 | `ShortTermInvestments`, `MarketableSecuritiesCurrent`,
55 | `AvailableForSaleSecuritiesDebtSecuritiesCurrent`. All four appear in the 12 name test
56 | set, and Ionis uses two at once.
57 | 
58 | Part of the 45% is genuine (small companies hold only cash) and part is fragmentation.
59 | Either way the missing half skews toward the well capitalized companies whose runway is
60 | most interesting.
61 | 
62 | **Fix:** sum across all securities tags, but only those struck on the same date as the
63 | cash balance, or the numerator mixes two quarters. Record which path resolved.
64 | 
65 | ### 1.5 `company_tickers.json` is missing live filers
66 | 
67 | The file is complete (10,426 entries, 870 KB) and Amicus Therapeutics (FOLD) is simply
68 | not in it. Neither is Verve.
69 | 
70 | **Fix:** the ticker map is a demo convenience only. The universe comes from the DERA
71 | Financial Statement Data Sets quarterly ZIP, keyed on CIK and SIC code, where no ticker
72 | is involved. Use SIC 2834, 2835, 2836. SIC 8731 is a red herring: 15 filings against
73 | 412 at 2834.
74 | 
75 | ### 1.6 Operating cash flow is not burn for a company with revenue
76 | 
77 | Arrowhead's trailing year contains an $825M partnership inflow, netting its burn to near
78 | zero and computing a **1,116 month runway**. Arithmetically correct, financially
79 | meaningless.
80 | 
81 | **Fix:** flag any cash-positive operating quarter in the trailing window and any case
82 | where the two burn estimates disagree by more than 3x. Flagged rows stay visible with
83 | the reason attached but never carry a rank. On the correct population (clinical-stage,
84 | pre-revenue) the flag rate went from 5 of 8 commercial names to 0 of 12.
85 | 
86 | ### 1.7 Sponsors revise dates backwards, and it is a typo
87 | 
88 | `NCT04613596` moved its completion date +1,317 days at version 92 and then -1,317 days
89 | at version 94, two months later. That is data entry, not a forecast revision. Any
90 | statistic over revision magnitudes needs a reversal filter.
91 | 
92 | ### 1.8 The sponsor to CIK join is the real engineering tax
93 | 
94 | `leadSponsor.name` is free text with no CIK. Forward matching (EDGAR name to registry)
95 | runs about 83% naively, and most misses are correct rejections (animal health, cannabis,
96 | shell companies). Sana and Intellia both failed to match in the demo. Realistic ceiling
97 | with an alias table plus hand review of the top 300 by market cap is 90-95%.
98 | 
99 | Budget a full day for this. It is the single largest unbudgeted cost in the project.
100 | 
101 | ### 1.9 `query.spons` matches collaborators, so a partner's trial becomes your catalyst
102 | 
103 | The v2 search parameter `query.spons` is not a lead-sponsor filter. It matches the
104 | sponsor/collaborator block as a whole, so a trial another company runs comes back under
105 | your issuer's name and `engine/gap.py` assigns it as that issuer's binding catalyst.
106 | 
107 | Observed: `find_trials("ARVINAS, INC.")` returns `NCT05654623` first. Its `leadSponsor`
108 | is **Pfizer**; Arvinas Estrogen Receptor, Inc. is a collaborator. Arvinas's funding gap
109 | was therefore computed against a Pfizer-run trial.
110 | 
111 | One of 8 joined names in the 12 name set. It does not error and it does not look wrong on
112 | screen, because the trial is real and the drug is genuinely theirs.
113 | 
114 | **Fix:** after searching, keep only trials whose `leadSponsor.name` matches the issuer,
115 | normalising the suffix noise (`Inc.`/`Inc`/`, Inc`/`AG`/`Corp`). The registry's lead
116 | sponsor string differs from EDGAR's registrant name in 3 of 8 joins even when correct
117 | (`Rocket Pharmaceuticals Inc.` vs `ROCKET PHARMACEUTICALS, INC.`), so match normalised,
118 | not literal.
119 | 
120 | ### 1.10 The nearest registered completion is usually the most stale one
121 | 
122 | `find_trials` sorts ascending by primary completion date and `build` takes `trials[0]`,
123 | which is the *earliest* date, not the *next* one. Because sponsors carry lapsed dates
124 | (finding 1.7 and the Rocket case), the earliest date is frequently already in the past,
125 | so the selection rule is biased toward exactly the stalest record in the set.
126 | 
127 | Three of 8 joined names picked an already-passed completion date on 2026-07-21: ARVN
128 | (2025-01-31), EDIT (2025-08), RCKT (2026-05-05).
129 | 
130 | This one is not cosmetic. Rocket is the demo's hero row. Against its picked date the gap
131 | is **+8.4 months, "funded to catalyst"**. Against its nearest *future* registered
132 | completion (`NCT06092034`, 2028-04) the same runway gives **-14.5 months, "financing
133 | required"**. Same company, same filing, opposite verdict, decided entirely by which
134 | registered date is treated as binding.
135 | 
136 | **Fix is a definition, not a patch, and it is Kristen's call:** decide whether the binding
137 | catalyst is the nearest completion date still in the future, or the nearest one whose
138 | trial is still live regardless of a lapsed date. The second is defensible and is arguably
139 | the project's whole point, but then the gap must be presented against a date the sponsor
140 | has already missed, and the demo has to say so out loud rather than print
141 | "funded to catalyst".
142 | 
143 | ## 2. Prior art, stated honestly
144 | 
145 | **The screen is not novel.** [BiopharmaWatch Catalyst Sync](https://www.biopharmawatch.com/catalyst-sync)
146 | sells exactly it: filter 11,000+ upcoming readouts by cash runway and burn rate across
147 | 949 companies. BioPharmCatalyst, Biomedtracker/Citeline, and Stifel's weekly biopharma
148 | update all occupy adjacent ground. EY Beyond Borders publishes the aggregate annually
149 | (33% of public biotechs under one year of runway at end 2025).
150 | 
151 | Do not pitch the screen. A judge finds BiopharmaWatch in one search.
152 | 
153 | **The revision panel is also not novel, and this was an overclaim in an earlier draft of
154 | this document.** [brbk/clinical_trials_history](https://huggingface.co/datasets/brbk/clinical_trials_history)
155 | is 4,333,631 rows across roughly 583,000 trials, every version, with
156 | `primary_completion_date` as a per-version field, built off the same internal endpoint.
157 | Do not say "nobody has assembled this."
158 | 
159 | Operationally this is good news: it removes the single largest engineering cost in the
160 | project. Crawling histories directly is roughly 50 to 100 requests per trial. Use the
161 | dataset for the panel and keep `engine/ctgov_history.py` for live single-trial checks in
162 | the demo, where fetching from the source is the point. Note the licence is CC-BY-NC-4.0,
163 | which covers a hackathon but not a commercial product.
164 | 
165 | **The finance-and-trial-timing link is adjacent, not empty.**
166 | [Guenzel & Liu, Excess Commitment in R&D, RFS 39(7) 2026](https://doi.org/10.1093/rfs/hhag026)
167 | uses clinical trial project data and finds that delays reduce subsequent project
168 | termination, instrumented with trial site congestion, moderated by CEO stock-price
169 | sensitivity. That is the opposite arrow from this project's hypothesis (delay causing
170 | firm behaviour, versus firm finances causing date revision behaviour), but it means the
171 | territory is occupied and "no paper connects finance and trial timing" is indefensible.
172 | Cite it, distinguish the arrow, and never claim empty terrain.
173 | 
174 | **What still appears open,** stated as a search result rather than a fact: we could not
175 | find work on sponsor liquidity predicting *disclosure* behaviour toward the registered
176 | date specifically, meaning how long a lapsed date is carried and how much notice a
177 | revision gives. That is narrower than "the intersection is unoccupied" and it is the
178 | most that can be honestly claimed without a systematic review.
179 | 
180 | Background that does exist: the trial delay literature
181 | ([Shadbolt et al., JAMA Netw Open 2023](https://jamanetwork.com/journals/jamanetworkopen/fullarticle/2800488),
182 | about 1 in 5 RCTs complete on time, median delay 12.2 months), the primary outcome
183 | switching literature, and the biotech financing literature
184 | ([Lerner, Shane & Tsai, JFE 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0304405X02002568)).
185 | 
186 | ## 3. The five hardest questions
187 | 
188 | **"Your burn number is wrong for exactly the companies that matter."**
189 | Correct. A Phase 3 ramp burns above the trailing rate; a partnership upfront shows a
190 | positive quarter. That is why burn is a band and unstable rows are excluded from
191 | ranking rather than silently included. Publish the sensitivity.
192 | 
193 | **"Primary completion date is not a readout."**
194 | Correct, and it is a systematic bias, not noise. PCD is last patient last visit for the
195 | primary endpoint; topline follows by weeks to months. The gap is optimistic by roughly
196 | 2 to 4 months on every row. The honest reframe is that the number is the sponsor's own
197 | registered expectation, which is weaker as a product and stronger as research, because
198 | the sponsor's stated belief is precisely the object of study.
199 | 
200 | **"Companies raise opportunistically, not at zero cash."**
201 | Concede fully. This is why "negative gap predicts financing" is close to tautological: a
202 | company with six months of cash raises within six months regardless. It also strengthens
203 | the real hypothesis, because if raising is window dependent then a company facing a
204 | closed window has a stronger reason to manage the visible date.
205 | 
206 | **"Survivorship."**
207 | Real. The public biotech universe shrank from 977 to 758 companies between 2021 and
208 | 2025, and the missing names are non randomly the negative gap tail. DERA is available
209 | per quarter back to 2009, so rebuild the universe as of each historical quarter. One
210 | afternoon, and it is the difference between a snapshot and a panel.
211 | 
212 | **"The interesting companies are the ones your arithmetic fails on."**
213 | Royalty monetization, non dilutive upfronts, debt facilities with milestone tranches,
214 | priority review voucher sales. Every one breaks cash divided by burn. This is the best
215 | justification for the model in the architecture: not to extract numbers, but to read the
216 | going concern and subsequent events footnotes and emit a categorical flag. Company
217 | guided runway versus computed runway is itself a chart worth showing.
218 |

174 | def _add_svg_x(revisions: list[dict]) -> list[dict]:
175 |     """Compute and attach svg_x to each revision dict (mutates in place, returns list).
176 | 
177 |     Maps each revision's submitted date linearly onto a 1100px canvas with 60px
178 |     padding each side.  When there is only one revision the single node sits centred.
179 |     """
180 |     dates = []
181 |     for r in revisions:
182 |         try:
183 |             dates.append(date.fromisoformat(r["submitted"]))
184 |         except (ValueError, TypeError):
185 |             dates.append(None)
186 | 
187 |     real_dates = [d for d in dates if d is not None]
188 |     if not real_dates:
189 |         for r in revisions:
190 |             r["svg_x"] = SVG_PAD + (SVG_WIDTH - 2 * SVG_PAD) / 2
191 |         return revisions
192 | 
193 |     span_start = min(real_dates)
194 |     span_end = max(real_dates)
195 |     total_days = (span_end - span_start).days or 1  # avoid div-by-zero for single node
196 | 
197 |     usable = SVG_WIDTH - 2 * SVG_PAD
198 |     for r, d in zip(revisions, dates):
199 |         if d is None:
200 |             r["svg_x"] = round(SVG_PAD + usable / 2, 1)
201 |         else:
202 |             frac = (d - span_start).days / total_days
203 |             r["svg_x"] = round(SVG_PAD + frac * usable, 1)
204 | 
205 |     return revisions

213 |     # carried_expired, days_expired and is_late_move are @property on Revision, so they
214 |     # are silently dropped.  Patch them in by reading the properties off the live objects
215 |     # rather than recomputing from held_days, so the snapshot cannot drift from the engine.
216 |     for rev_dict, rev_obj in zip(d["revisions"], history.revisions):
217 |         rev_dict["carried_expired"] = rev_obj.carried_expired
218 |         rev_dict["days_expired"] = rev_obj.days_expired
219 |         rev_dict["is_late_move"] = rev_obj.is_late_move
220 |     _add_svg_x(d["revisions"])
221 |     return d

256 | def _classify_once(delta, card) -> "ChallengeCard | None":  # type: ignore[name-defined]
257 |     """One attempt at Granite classification via run_redline.
258 | 
259 |     Returns None if the engine detected no breach — that is a broken premise,
260 |     not a case to work around.  Returns the ChallengeCard otherwise; caller
261 |     checks source.  A fresh GraniteClassifier is constructed each time so any
262 |     cached token state from a failed attempt does not carry over.
263 |     """
264 |     classifier = GraniteClassifier()
265 |     return run_redline(delta, card, classifier=classifier)

268 | def _build_rckt_redline(rckt: CatalystContract) -> dict:
269 |     """Construct a scripted +9-month shift on RCKT's catalyst_date and run the
270 |     redline loop with up to five attempts.  Exits non-zero if all fail."""
271 |     # Build the recomputed contract: same runway, same trial but with pcd shifted
272 |     # forward by RCKT_SHIFT_MONTHS months (approx 30.44 days/month).
273 |     original_pcd_str = rckt.trial["pcd"]
274 |     try:
275 |         original_date = date.fromisoformat(original_pcd_str)
276 |     except ValueError:

1 | """The catalyst contract: can this company fund itself to its own next readout?
2 | 
3 |     funding gap = runway exhaustion date - registered primary completion date
4 | 
5 | Negative means the money runs out first. Both sides are deterministic and sourced:
6 | the left from SEC XBRL tags, the right from the sponsor's own registry filing. No
7 | model contributes a number to either.
8 | 
9 | What makes this more than a subtraction is the third column. The registry date is not
10 | an observation, it is a *claim the sponsor can revise at will*, and every revision is
11 | timestamped. So alongside the gap we carry the sponsor's revision behaviour: how many
12 | times the date moved, and how much notice was given each time. A company that pushes a
13 | readout eighteen months out with a year of warning is forecasting. One that moves it
14 | three weeks before it arrives was showing a date it had stopped believing -- and if
15 | that behaviour turns out to cluster in cash-constrained sponsors, the registry is not
16 | a data source, it is a disclosure channel with incentives.
17 | 
18 | That last claim is a hypothesis, not a result. This module assembles the panel needed
19 | to test it. It does not test it, and nothing here should be presented as if it had.
20 | """
21 | from __future__ import annotations
22 | 
23 | import json
24 | import urllib.parse
25 | from dataclasses import dataclass, field
26 | from datetime import date
27 | 
28 | from engine.ctgov_history import TrialHistory, _get, _parse_date, fetch_history
29 | from engine.runway import Runway, compute_runway, ticker_to_cik
30 | 
31 | V2 = "https://clinicaltrials.gov/api/v2/studies"
32 | # Phase 2/3 only. Early-phase trials are not value-inflection points, and including
33 | # them buries the catalyst in a pile of dose-escalation studies.
34 | PIVOTAL_PHASES = {"PHASE2", "PHASE3", "PHASE2_PHASE3"}
35 | LIVE = {"RECRUITING", "ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING", "ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION", "NOT_YET_RECRUITING"}
36 | 
37 | 
38 | def find_trials(sponsor: str, limit: int = 40) -> list[dict]:
39 |     """Live pivotal-ish trials for one lead sponsor.
40 | 
41 |     The sponsor field is free text with no CIK, so this join is the engineering tax
42 |     of the whole project: matching is by name, and names drift (subsidiaries, "Inc."
43 |     vs "Inc", post-merger renames). Good enough to demo one company; a universe run
44 |     needs an alias table and hand review of the largest names.
45 |     """
46 |     q = urllib.parse.urlencode({
47 |         "query.spons": sponsor,
48 |         "fields": ",".join([
49 |             "protocolSection.identificationModule.nctId",
50 |             "protocolSection.identificationModule.briefTitle",
51 |             "protocolSection.statusModule.overallStatus",
52 |             "protocolSection.statusModule.primaryCompletionDateStruct",
53 |             "protocolSection.designModule.phases",
54 |             "protocolSection.sponsorCollaboratorsModule.leadSponsor",
55 |         ]),
56 |         "pageSize": limit,
57 |     })
58 |     out = []
59 |     for s in _get(f"{V2}?{q}").get("studies", []):
60 |         p = s.get("protocolSection", {})
61 |         phases = set(p.get("designModule", {}).get("phases", []) or [])
62 |         status = p.get("statusModule", {}).get("overallStatus", "")
63 |         pcd = p.get("statusModule", {}).get("primaryCompletionDateStruct", {}) or {}
64 |         if not (phases & PIVOTAL_PHASES) or status not in LIVE or not pcd.get("date"):
65 |             continue
66 |         out.append({
67 |             "nct": p["identificationModule"]["nctId"],
68 |             "title": p["identificationModule"].get("briefTitle", ""),
69 |             "status": status,
70 |             "pcd": pcd["date"],
71 |             "pcd_type": pcd.get("type", ""),
72 |             "phases": sorted(phases),
73 |             "sponsor": p.get("sponsorCollaboratorsModule", {}).get("leadSponsor", {}).get("name", ""),
74 |         })
75 |     return sorted(out, key=lambda t: t["pcd"])
76 | 
77 | 
78 | @dataclass
79 | class CatalystContract:
80 |     """One testable claim: this company reaches this readout on this money."""
81 |     runway: Runway
82 |     trial: dict
83 |     history: TrialHistory | None = None
84 |     # Pivotal trials whose registered completion has already passed. Never the
85 |     # catalyst, always kept: a lapsed date is the integrity signal, not a target.
86 |     lapsed: list[dict] = field(default_factory=list)
87 |     lapsed_history: list[TrialHistory] = field(default_factory=list)
88 | 
89 |     @property
90 |     def catalyst_date(self) -> date | None:
91 |         return _parse_date(self.trial["pcd"])
92 | 
93 |     @property
94 |     def gap_months(self) -> float | None:
95 |         """Months of runway left at the registered readout date. Negative = short.
96 | 
97 |         Computed against the CONSERVATIVE end of the burn band. Reporting the
98 |         optimistic end would flatter every row in the same direction.
99 |         """
100 |         end = self.runway.exhaustion(self.runway.months_low)
101 |         cat = self.catalyst_date
102 |         if end is None or cat is None:
103 |             return None
104 |         return (end - cat).days / (365.25 / 12)
105 | 
106 |     @property
107 |     def verdict(self) -> str:
108 |         g = self.gap_months
109 |         if g is None:
110 |             return "not computable"
111 |         if not self.runway.reliable:
112 |             return "not rankable (burn estimate unreliable)"
113 |         return "funded to catalyst" if g >= 0 else "financing required before catalyst"
114 | 
115 |     def lines(self) -> list[str]:
116 |         r, t = self.runway, self.trial
117 |         g = self.gap_months
118 |         out = [
119 |             f"{r.ticker}  {r.name}",
120 |             f"  liquidity      ${r.liquidity/1e6:,.0f}M  ({r.provenance['cash']} + {r.provenance['securities']}, as of {r.as_of})",
121 |             f"  burn band      ${r.burn_ttm_annual/1e6:,.0f}M/yr trailing, ${r.burn_recent_annual/1e6:,.0f}M/yr most recent quarter",
122 |             f"  runway         {r.months_low:.1f}-{r.months_high:.1f} months, exhausted {r.exhaustion(r.months_low)}",
123 |             f"  catalyst       {t['nct']}  {'/'.join(t['phases'])}  {t['status']}",
124 |             f"                 {t['title'][:70]}",
125 |             f"                 primary completion {t['pcd']} ({t['pcd_type'].lower()})",
126 |             f"  FUNDING GAP    {'n/a' if g is None else f'{g:+.1f} months'}   -> {self.verdict}",
127 |         ]
128 |         if self.history and self.history.revisions:
129 |             h = self.history
130 |             out += [
131 |                 f"  date integrity {len(h.revisions)} revisions across {h.n_versions} versions, "
132 |                 f"net slip {h.total_slip_days:+d} days, {h.n_late_moves} moved with under 90 days notice",
133 |             ]
134 |             if h.n_expired_carried:
135 |                 out.append(f"  *** EXPIRED     showed an already-passed completion date for up to "
136 |                            f"{h.max_days_expired} days before correcting it")
137 |             for rev in h.revisions[-4:]:
138 |                 if rev.carried_expired:
139 |                     notice = f"{rev.days_expired:>4}d EXPIRED"
140 |                 elif rev.held_days is None:
141 |                     notice = ""
142 |                 else:
143 |                     notice = f"{rev.held_days:>5}d notice"
144 |                 moved = "" if rev.moved_days is None else f"{rev.moved_days:+5d}d"
145 |                 out.append(f"                 v{rev.version:<4} {rev.submitted}  -> {rev.pcd}  {moved}  {notice}")
146 |         for t in self.lapsed:
147 |             out.append(f"  LAPSED         {t['nct']}  primary completion {t['pcd']} has already passed, "
148 |                        f"not treated as a catalyst")
149 |         for h in self.lapsed_history:
150 |             if h.n_expired_carried:
151 |                 out.append(f"  *** EXPIRED     {h.nct} showed an already-passed completion date for up to "
152 |                            f"{h.max_days_expired} days before correcting it")
153 |         for n in r.notes:
154 |             out.append(f"  note           {n}")
155 |         return out
156 | 
157 | 
158 | def _history_or_none(nct: str) -> TrialHistory | None:
159 |     try:
160 |         return fetch_history(nct)
161 |     except (RuntimeError, ValueError):
162 |         return None  # history is an enrichment, not a precondition
163 | 
164 | 
165 | def build(ticker: str, cik_map=None, with_history: bool = True,
166 |           as_of: date | None = None) -> CatalystContract | None:
167 |     r = compute_runway(ticker, cik_map)
168 |     trials = find_trials(r.name)
169 |     if not trials:
170 |         return None
171 | 
172 |     # The binding catalyst is the nearest registered primary completion STILL IN THE
173 |     # FUTURE. Taking trials[0] off a date-ascending list bound RCKT to a date that had
174 |     # lapsed 77 days earlier, which made its gap read +8.4 months "funded to catalyst"
175 |     # when the nearest real catalyst put it 14.5 months short. You cannot run out of
176 |     # money before an event that already happened.
177 |     #
178 |     # Lapsed pivotal trials are not dropped. They are the date-integrity signal this
179 |     # project exists to surface, so they ride along on `lapsed`.
180 |     today = as_of or date.today()
181 |     future, lapsed = [], []
182 |     for t in trials:
183 |         d = _parse_date(t["pcd"])
184 |         (future if d is not None and d >= today else lapsed).append(t)
185 | 
186 |     if not future:
187 |         # Every pivotal date this sponsor has registered is in the past. There is no
188 |         # catalyst to be funded to, and inventing one from a lapsed date is the bug
189 |         # above. The lapsed trials are still worth surfacing, so say so and stop.
190 |         return None
191 | 
192 |     trial = future[0]
193 |     hist = _history_or_none(trial["nct"]) if with_history else None
194 |     lapsed_hist = [_history_or_none(t["nct"]) for t in lapsed] if with_history else []
195 |     return CatalystContract(runway=r, trial=trial, history=hist,
196 |                             lapsed=lapsed, lapsed_history=[h for h in lapsed_hist if h])
197 |

199 | def demo() -> None:

201 |     built = 0
202 |     for ticker in ["SANA", "PRME", "RCKT", "NTLA"]:
203 |         c = build(ticker, cik_map)
204 |         if c is None:
205 |             print(f"{ticker}: no live pivotal trial matched by sponsor name\n")
206 |             continue
207 |         built += 1
208 |         assert c.runway.ticker == ticker
209 |         assert c.trial["nct"].startswith("NCT")
210 |         # A lapsed completion date is never a catalyst. You cannot run out of money
211 |         # before an event that already happened, so a bound date in the past makes
212 |         # gap_months meaningless and flatters the row. Lapsed pivotal trials are
213 |         # retained on `c.lapsed` as date-integrity signals instead.
214 |         assert c.catalyst_date is None or c.catalyst_date >= date.today(), (
215 |             f"{ticker} bound to a lapsed catalyst {c.catalyst_date} "
216 |             f"({c.trial['nct']})")
217 |         if c.gap_months is not None:
218 |             # Gap must equal exhaustion minus catalyst, to the day.
219 |             recomputed = (c.runway.exhaustion(c.runway.months_low) - c.catalyst_date).days
220 |             assert abs(recomputed / (365.25 / 12) - c.gap_months) < 1e-9
221 |         print("\n".join(c.lines()))

Contents of file orchestrator/redline.py:

1 | """Redline: detect when a recomputed CatalystContract breaches its approved belief,
2 | then build a ChallengeCard for human review.
3 | 
4 | Entry point: `run_redline(approved_contract, new_contract, card, classifier)`.
5 | 
6 | The engine produces two metric packets -- one for the previously approved state of the
7 | contract, one for the recomputed state after an amendment or new filing. `as_directions`
8 | turns the per-metric change into a description suitable for Granite: the direction and
9 | size of the move in words, never a measured value. The application renders every figure.
10 | 
11 | Metric definitions (required because the signs are non-obvious):
12 |     gap_months           signed months of runway surplus at the registered primary
13 |                          completion date; negative means cash is exhausted BEFORE the
14 |                          readout, not that the trial is running late.
15 |     runway_months_low    months until cash exhaustion at the conservative (high-burn)
16 |                          end of the burn band.
17 |     burn_ttm_annual      trailing-twelve-month annualised operating cash outflow, in
18 |                          dollars; higher is worse.
19 |     pcd_revisions        cumulative count of times the registry date has moved.
20 |     max_days_expired     longest continuous stretch, in days, that the registry showed
21 |                          a primary completion date that had already passed.
22 | """
23 | from __future__ import annotations
24 | 
25 | from dataclasses import dataclass
26 | 
27 | from engine.contract import to_packet
28 | from engine.gap import CatalystContract
29 | from engine.ledger import BeliefCard, Breach
30 | from orchestrator.challenge import ChallengeCard, build_challenge
31 | from orchestrator.classifier import StubClassifier
32 | 
33 | # Metric definitions surfaced in the brief so the model knows what the sign means.
34 | # Indexed by metric_id; each value is the one-line definition.
35 | _METRIC_DEFS: dict[str, str] = {
36 |     "gap_months": (
37 |         "signed months of runway surplus at the registered primary completion date; "
38 |         "negative means cash is exhausted BEFORE the readout, not that the trial is "
39 |         "running late"
40 |     ),
41 |     "runway_months_low": (
42 |         "months until cash exhaustion at the conservative end of the burn band; "
43 |         "lower is worse"
44 |     ),
45 |     "burn_ttm_annual": (
46 |         "trailing-twelve-month annualised operating cash outflow in dollars; "
47 |         "higher means the company is spending more"
48 |     ),
49 |     "pcd_revisions": (
50 |         "cumulative number of times the sponsor has revised the registered primary "
51 |         "completion date; a rising count means the sponsor is revising more"
52 |     ),
53 |     "max_days_expired": (
54 |         "longest continuous stretch in days that the registry showed a completion date "
55 |         "that had already passed; higher means the sponsor carried a stale date longer"
56 |     ),
57 | }
58 | 
59 | 
60 | def _direction(pct_change: float) -> str:
61 |     """Direction and magnitude of a metric shift, in words.
62 | 
63 |     Buckets are deliberately coarse -- the model reasons over these, so they must
64 |     not be reconstructible into a figure. The same vocabulary as scenario.py's
65 |     _direction, adapted for metrics that move in both directions.
66 |     """
67 |     if pct_change != pct_change:    # nan
68 |         return "not comparable"
69 |     verb = "rises" if pct_change > 0 else "falls"
70 |     mag = abs(pct_change)
71 |     if mag < 1:
72 |         return "essentially unchanged"
73 |     if mag < 5:
74 |         return f"{verb} slightly"
75 |     if mag < 15:
76 |         return f"{verb} moderately"
77 |     if mag < 30:
78 |         return f"{verb} materially"
79 |     return f"{verb} sharply"
80 | 
81 | 
82 | def _pct_change(before: float, after: float) -> float:
83 |     """Signed percentage change. Returns nan when the denominator is zero."""
84 |     if before == 0.0:
85 |         return float("nan")
86 |     return (after / before - 1.0) * 100
87 | 
88 | 
89 | def as_directions(before: dict[str, float], after: dict[str, float]) -> str:
90 |     """Express each metric change as a direction label, never a value.
91 | 
92 |     Only metrics present in both packets are reported. Each line names the metric,
93 |     its one-line definition (so the model knows what the sign means), and the
94 |     direction of the move.
95 | 
96 |     The application renders every number. This string contains none.
97 |     """
98 |     lines = []
99 |     for metric in sorted(set(before) & set(after)):
100 |         b, a = before[metric], after[metric]
101 |         direction = _direction(_pct_change(b, a))
102 |         defn = _METRIC_DEFS.get(metric, "")
103 |         if defn:
104 |             lines.append(f"  {metric} ({defn}): {direction}")
105 |         else:
106 |             lines.append(f"  {metric}: {direction}")
107 |     return "\n".join(lines) if lines else "  no shared metrics changed"
108 | 
109 | 
110 | @dataclass
111 | class ContractDelta:
112 |     """Before and after packets for one amendment event."""
113 |     approved: CatalystContract    # the contract as it was when last approved
114 |     recomputed: CatalystContract  # the contract after the new filing or amendment
115 | 
116 |     @property
117 |     def before(self) -> dict[str, float]:
118 |         return to_packet(self.approved)
119 | 
120 |     @property
121 |     def after(self) -> dict[str, float]:
122 |         return to_packet(self.recomputed)
123 | 
124 |     def directions(self) -> str:
125 |         """What moved, in words. Fed to Granite; never contains a measured value."""
126 |         return as_directions(self.before, self.after)
127 | 
128 | 
129 | def _breach_for_gap(card: BeliefCard, new_packet: dict[str, float]) -> Breach | None:
130 |     """Return the gap_months breach if one exists, else None.
131 | 
132 |     The funding gap is the primary signal. When it is absent (not computable) the
133 |     breach cannot be constructed and the caller falls back to other metrics.
134 |     """
135 |     gap = new_packet.get("gap_months")
136 |     if gap is None:
137 |         return None
138 |     if card.in_range(gap):
139 |         return None
140 |     return Breach(
141 |         card_id=card.card_id,
142 |         metric="gap_months",
143 |         observed=gap,
144 |         expected_low=card.expected_low,
145 |         expected_high=card.expected_high,
146 |         direction="over" if gap > card.expected_high else "under",
147 |     )
148 | 
149 | 
150 | def run_redline(
151 |     delta: ContractDelta,
152 |     card: BeliefCard,
153 |     classifier=None,
154 |     context: dict | None = None,
155 | ) -> ChallengeCard | None:
156 |     """Build a ChallengeCard when a recomputed contract breaches the approved belief.
157 | 
158 |     Returns None when no breach is detected -- the contract still satisfies the
159 |     approved range and no review is needed.
160 | 
161 |     `context` is forwarded to Granite as supplementary evidence (e.g. news snippets).
162 |     The directions string is merged into it so the prompt carries both the standing
163 |     belief and a description of what moved.
164 | 
165 |     The classifier defaults to StubClassifier so the loop is demoable without
166 |     credentials; swap in a GraniteClassifier for live judgment.
167 |     """
168 |     classifier = classifier or StubClassifier()
169 | 
170 |     breach = _breach_for_gap(card, delta.after)
171 |     if breach is None:
172 |         return None
173 | 
174 |     # Merge the directions summary into context so the Granite user prompt carries
175 |     # the description of what moved alongside the standing belief and breach reading.
176 |     ctx = dict(context or {})
177 |     ctx["directions"] = delta.directions()
178 | 
179 |     return build_challenge(card, breach, ctx, classifier)
180 |

1 | """Tests for the Catalyst Integrity Desk console.
2 | 
3 | No live server needed — uses Flask app.test_client().
4 | No network access — all data comes from data/snapshot.json.
5 | 
6 | Number-provenance invariant (spec sub-task 6, step 8)
7 | ------------------------------------------------------
8 | Before finalising this suite, a literal "9999" was temporarily added to a
9 | <td> in detail.html and the provenance test was run. It failed with:
10 | 
11 |     AssertionError: number token '9999' from GET /contract/RCKT not in snapshot
12 | 
13 | The hardcoded value was then removed. A provenance check that has never been
14 | seen failing is not evidence of anything.
15 | 
16 | Badge-tamper test (Prompt 3 repair, item 1)
17 | -------------------------------------------
18 | Before adding the fix to redline_confirm, this test was run and failed with:
19 | 
20 |     AssertionError: badge should read 'tampered' after a hashed byte is edited
21 | 
22 | That confirmed the confirm handler was reading intact from the query string
23 | rather than calling verify() itself.
24 | """
25 | from __future__ import annotations
26 | 
27 | import json
28 | import os
29 | import re
30 | import sys
31 | import tempfile
32 | from html.parser import HTMLParser
33 | 
34 | import pytest
35 | 
36 | # Ensure the repo root is importable.
37 | sys.path.insert(0, os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), ".."))
38 | 
39 | from console.app import app as flask_app, DECISIONS_PATH
40 | 
41 | SNAPSHOT_PATH = os.path.join(os.path.dirname(__file__), "..", "data", "snapshot.json")
42 | 
43 | 
44 | # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
45 | # Fixtures
46 | # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
47 | 
48 | @pytest.fixture(scope="session")
49 | def client():
50 |     flask_app.config["TESTING"] = True
51 |     with flask_app.test_client() as c:
52 |         yield c
53 | 
54 | 
55 | @pytest.fixture(scope="session")
56 | def snapshot_raw() -> str:
57 |     """Raw snapshot JSON as a string — used for substring matching."""
58 |     with open(SNAPSHOT_PATH) as f:
59 |         return f.read()

115 | # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
116 | # Number-provenance invariant
117 | # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
118 | 
119 | class _TextCollector(HTMLParser):
120 |     """Walk all text nodes (not attribute values) and collect visible text."""
121 | 
122 |     def __init__(self):
123 |         super().__init__()
124 |         self._skip = False
125 |         self.chunks: list[str] = []
126 | 
127 |     def handle_starttag(self, tag, attrs):
128 |         # style and script content is not user-visible text.
129 |         if tag in ("style", "script"):
130 |             self._skip = True
131 | 
132 |     def handle_endtag(self, tag):
133 |         if tag in ("style", "script"):
134 |             self._skip = False
135 | 
136 |     def handle_data(self, data):
137 |         if not self._skip:
138 |             self.chunks.append(data)
139 | 
140 | 
141 | _TOKEN_RE = re.compile(r"-?\d+(?:\.\d+)?")
142 | 
143 | 
144 | def _visible_numbers(html: bytes) -> list[str]:
145 |     """Extract every number-like token from the visible text of an HTML page."""
146 |     parser = _TextCollector()
147 |     parser.feed(html.decode())
148 |     text = " ".join(parser.chunks)
149 |     return _TOKEN_RE.findall(text)
150 |

153 | # Integrity badge — tamper detection (Prompt 3 repair, item 1)
154 | # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
155 | 
156 | def test_badge_flips_on_tampered_ledger(tmp_path, monkeypatch):
157 |     """POST a decision, tamper a byte inside the card payload (which the hash
158 |     covers), reload /redline/confirm, and assert the badge reads 'tampered'.
159 | 
160 |     The verdict word lives in review_log.jsonl, not the ledger, so editing
161 |     'approve' there changes nothing hashed and verify() stays True.  The tamper
162 |     must touch something inside the 'card' payload of a ledger entry.
163 |     """
164 |     decisions_file = str(tmp_path / "decisions.jsonl")
165 |     review_log_file = str(tmp_path / "review_log.jsonl")
166 | 
167 |     # Point the app at the temp files for this test.
168 |     monkeypatch.setattr("console.app.DECISIONS_PATH", decisions_file)
169 |     monkeypatch.setattr("console.app.REVIEW_LOG_PATH", review_log_file)
170 | 
171 |     flask_app.config["TESTING"] = True
172 |     with flask_app.test_client() as c:
173 |         # POST an approve decision to populate the ledger.
174 |         resp = c.post("/redline/decide", data={"verdict": "approve", "reason": "test"})
175 |         assert resp.status_code == 302
176 |         assert "verdict=approve" in resp.headers["Location"]
177 | 
178 |         # Confirm page should show intact before tamper.
179 |         resp = c.get("/redline/confirm?verdict=approve")
180 |         assert b"intact" in resp.data
181 |         assert b"tampered" not in resp.data
182 | 
183 |         # Tamper: change a character inside the card payload of the first ledger entry.
184 |         # The verdict word is in review_log.jsonl, not here, so we must edit the card.
185 |         with open(decisions_file) as f:
186 |             lines = f.readlines()
187 |         assert lines, "ledger should have at least one entry after approve"
188 |         # Replace a digit in the first entry's card payload so the hash check fails.
189 |         tampered = lines[0].replace('"version": 1', '"version": 2', 1)
190 |         assert tampered != lines[0], "tamper must change something"
191 |         with open(decisions_file, "w") as f:
192 |             f.write(tampered)
193 |             f.writelines(lines[1:])
194 | 
195 |         # Reload confirm — the handler must call verify() fresh, not trust the URL param.
196 |         resp = c.get("/redline/confirm?verdict=approve")
197 |         assert b"tampered" in resp.data, (
198 |             "badge should read 'tampered' after a hashed byte is edited"
199 |         )
200 |

203 | # Flagged row — SRPT visible but unranked (Prompt 3 widening, item 3)
204 | # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
205 | 
206 | def test_contracts_flagged_section_contains_srpt(client):
207 |     """SRPT must appear in the flagged section of /contracts."""
208 |     r = client.get("/contracts")
209 |     assert r.status_code == 200
210 |     assert b"SRPT" in r.data, "/contracts must show SRPT in the flagged section"
211 | 
212 | 
213 | def test_srpt_has_no_rank_number(client):
214 |     """SRPT is unreliable and must not carry a rank number on /contracts.
215 | 
216 |     The ranked table uses loop.index (1, 2, 3, ...) in the Rank column.
217 |     SRPT must appear in the flagged table, which has no Rank column.
218 |     We verify this by checking the HTML: SRPT's row must not be preceded
219 |     by a rank cell containing a digit within the same <tr>.
220 | 
221 |     Implementation: parse the page, find every <tr> that contains 'SRPT',
222 |     and assert none of them also contains a lone digit that would be a rank.
223 |     """
224 |     from html.parser import HTMLParser as _HP
225 | 
226 |     class _TableParser(_HP):
227 |         def __init__(self):
228 |             super().__init__()
229 |             self._in_tr = False
230 |             self._cells: list[str] = []
231 |             self._cur: list[str] = []
232 |             self.srpt_rows: list[list[str]] = []  # all <tr> cells containing SRPT
233 | 
234 |         def handle_starttag(self, tag, attrs):
235 |             if tag == "tr":
236 |                 self._in_tr = True
237 |                 self._cells = []
238 |                 self._cur = []
239 | 
240 |         def handle_endtag(self, tag):
241 |             if tag == "td":
242 |                 self._cells.append("".join(self._cur).strip())
243 |                 self._cur = []
244 |             elif tag == "tr":
245 |                 row_text = " ".join(self._cells)
246 |                 if "SRPT" in row_text:
247 |                     self.srpt_rows.append(list(self._cells))
248 |                 self._in_tr = False
249 | 
250 |         def handle_data(self, data):
251 |             if self._in_tr:
252 |                 self._cur.append(data)
253 | 
254 |     r = client.get("/contracts")
255 |     p = _TableParser()
256 |     p.feed(r.data.decode())
257 |     assert p.srpt_rows, "SRPT must appear in at least one table row"
258 |     for row in p.srpt_rows:
259 |         # A rank cell is a cell whose stripped content is a plain integer.
260 |         rank_cells = [c for c in row if c.isdigit()]
261 |         assert not rank_cells, (
262 |             f"SRPT row contains a rank cell {rank_cells!r} — "
263 |             "unreliable rows must not be ranked"
264 |         )

268 | def test_number_provenance(client, snapshot_raw, route):
269 |     """Every number visible in the rendered HTML must appear in snapshot.json.
270 | 
271 |     This is the mechanical check for the 'no number displayed to a user was
272 |     computed in the view layer' invariant.  Attribute values (viewBox, r,
273 |     stroke-width, etc.) are not text nodes and are not reached by the parser.
274 |     SVG x positions are stored in the snapshot (sub-task 1) and pass on their
275 |     own merit.
276 | 
277 |     Failure verification (performed before finalising — see module docstring):
278 |     a literal '9999' was temporarily added to a <td> in detail.html and this
279 |     test correctly failed, naming '9999' as the offending token on
280 |     GET /contract/RCKT.
281 |     """
282 |     r = client.get(route)
283 |     assert r.status_code == 200
284 |     tokens = _visible_numbers(r.data)
285 |     for token in tokens:
286 |         assert token in snapshot_raw, (
287 |             f"number token {token!r} from GET {route} not found in snapshot.json\n"
288 |             f"(This means a number was computed or hardcoded in the view layer.)"
289 |         )
290 |

293 | # Snapshot staleness guard (Prompt 3, the breach moment, item 1)
294 | # ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
295 | 
296 | def test_snapshot_no_lapsed_catalyst():
297 |     """No contract in the snapshot may bind to a catalyst date in the past.
298 | 
299 |     The stale snapshot passed every existing test while reporting RCKT as
300 |     'funded to catalyst' against NCT04248439 (registered primary completion
301 |     2026-05-05, a date already in the past).  This check is the one that
302 |     would have caught it.
303 | 
304 |     The engine's build() now enforces the same rule: it binds to the nearest
305 |     registered primary completion still in the future and puts lapsed trials
306 |     on contract.lapsed.  The snapshot must reflect that rule.
307 | 
308 |     Failure verification: this test was written before the snapshot was
309 |     rebuilt.  With the stale snapshot it failed on RCKT with catalyst_date
310 |     2026-05-05, which is in the past.
311 |     """
312 |     import datetime as _dt
313 | 
314 |     with open(SNAPSHOT_PATH) as f:
315 |         snap = json.load(f)
316 | 
317 |     today = _dt.date.today().isoformat()
318 |     for ticker, c in snap["contracts"].items():
319 |         cat = c.get("catalyst_date")
320 |         if cat is None:
321 |             continue  # no catalyst is fine; no catalyst != past catalyst
322 |         assert cat >= today, (
323 |             f"{ticker} binds to catalyst_date {cat!r} which is in the past "
324 |             f"(today is {today}).  The snapshot was built against a lapsed date "
325 |             f"and must be regenerated."
326 |         )
